description: This figure contains two log-log plots displaying oxidation kinetics of Fe-Mn-Al based alloys oxidized at 800 °C in 200 torr pure oxygen for up to 24 hours, with x-axis as weight gain (mg·cm⁻²) and y-axis as oxidation time (hrs). Subplot (a) presents data for alloys that form protective alumina scales, each curve is marked with the oxidation kinetic exponent n derived from its slope, ranging from 2 to 5.6, featuring relatively low weight gain and stable oxidation rate. Subplot (b) shows data for alloys with breakaway and healing oxidation kinetics, with higher total weight gain and inflection points on curves corresponding to oxide scale rupture and subsequent reformation. The comparison confirms that alloys forming continuous protective alumina scales have much lower oxidation weight gain, indicating better high-temperature oxidation resistance, while varied kinetic exponents reflect different oxidation rate-controlling mechanisms.

This figure consists of two subplots illustrating the 24-hour isothermal oxidation weight gain ΔW/24 (unit: mg·cm⁻²) of Fe-Mn-Al alloys oxidized at 800°C in pure oxygen, as a function of alloy elemental composition. Subplot (a) presents ΔW/24 against Mn concentration (0 to 30 wt.%) for alloys with fixed aluminum contents (6Al, 7Al, 8Al, 9Al series). Subplot (b) presents ΔW/24 against Al concentration (5 to 17.5 wt.%) for alloys with fixed manganese contents (2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20 wt.% Mn series). Key observable trends include: for fixed Al levels, oxidation weight gain reaches a minimum when Mn content ranges 5~10 wt.%; for fixed Mn levels, minimum weight gain occurs at Al content of 6~9 wt.%, while weight gain rises as Al content exceeds 9 wt.% attributed to Mn-rich oxide nodules penetrating the protective alumina scale. This figure supports the core conclusion that fully ferritic Fe-(5-10)wt.% Mn-(6-10)wt.% Al alloys exhibit optimal 800°C oxidation resistance, with alloy composition dominating oxidation rate via regulating the morphology and phase constitution of the formed oxide scale.

This is an 800°C isothermal oxide map of the Fe-Mn-Al ternary alloy system, superimposing oxidation scale morphology regions on the alloy phase equilibrium section. The triangular plot uses weight percent as units, with the bottom axis representing manganese content (0 to 40 wt.%) and left axis representing aluminum content (0 to 25 wt.%). Four composition regions with distinct scale morphologies are marked: Region I forms alumina with bulky oxide nodules, Region II forms alumina with bulky Mn-rich oxides, Region III forms continuous protective alumina scales (corresponding to the most oxidation-resistant fully ferritic alloys with Fe-(5–10) wt.% Mn-(6–10) wt.% Al composition), Region IV forms alumina with small Mn-rich oxide nodules. Alloy phase regions including α (ferrite), α+γ (ferrite + austenite), γ (austenite), α+β-Mn, γ+β-Mn are also labeled. The map demonstrates that austenite phase impairs oxidation resistance, and correlates alloy composition, matrix phase, and oxidation performance to guide composition design for high-temperature oxidation-resistant Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

This micrograph shows the cross-section of a large oxide nodule formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 800 °C under 200 torr pure oxygen; the caption specifies the alloy composition as Fe-7.5Mn-3Al, while associated supporting text cites this figure for oxide nodules formed on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al alloy. The bright region at the lower left is the Fe-Mn-Al alloy substrate, the dark upper region is the embedding mounting medium, and the heterogeneous, porous layered structure between them is the grown oxide nodule with visible internal voids distributed across the oxide layer. Text reports this nodule is structurally similar to nodules formed on binary Fe-Al alloys, consisting of an outer white Fe₂O₃ layer, an inner Fe₃O₄ layer, with Mn and Al enrichment detected in the inner Fe₃O₄ layer and at the gas-oxide interface, plus Mn₂O₃ particles present in the outer Fe₂O₃ layer. This figure supports that oxide nodules formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloys with insufficient Al content to form continuous protective alumina scales have phase compositions strongly dependent on Mn alloying content.

This figure contains two subgraphs targeting oxide nodules formed on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al alloy after high-temperature oxidation. The first subgraph is an EDS elemental mapping image, demonstrating the spatial distribution of Mn and Al within the oxide nodule, with clear enrichment signals of these two elements in specific regions of the scale. The second subgraph is a cross-sectional scanning electron micrograph, showing the layered structure of the oxide nodule, internal pores, irregular outer surface morphology, and the interface between the alloy substrate and oxide scale. The results verify that Mn and Al are remarkably enriched in the inner Fe₃O₄ layer and the gas-oxide interface of the nodule, supporting the conclusion that Mn accumulates at the outer oxide surface of Fe-Mn-Al alloys with Mn content higher than ~5 wt% during oxidation, providing morphological and compositional evidence for the high-temperature oxidation mechanism of Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

Caption and associated research text state this is an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) X-ray elemental mapping image of oxide nodules formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy after isothermal high temperature oxidation under 200 torr pure oxygen. The bright regions on the dark background correspond to the enrichment area of the detected target element. The mapping proves notable enrichment of manganese and aluminum in the Fe₃O₄ inner layer and at the gas-oxide interface of the oxide scale, which matches the observation that alloys with Mn content higher than ~5 wt% form a semi-continuous Mn-rich outer oxide layer during oxidation. This figure provides direct evidence of element migration during oxidation, and supports the conclusion that oxide scale composition of Fe-Mn-Al alloys is strongly dependent on their Mn and Al contents.

This is an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental X-ray map (part of the Fig. 5 series) for the cross-section of oxide scale formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy after isothermal high-temperature oxidation under 200 torr pure oxygen. As reported in the associated text, the map demonstrates significant enrichment of Mn and Al both in the inner Fe₃O₄ layer of the oxide nodules and at the gas-oxide interface. For alloys containing more than ~5 wt% Mn, the observed Mn enrichment at the gas-oxide interface corresponds to a thin, semi-continuous outer grey oxide layer. This elemental distribution result confirms the Mn and Al migration behavior during oxidation, and supports the conclusion that Mn concentration is a key factor determining the phase composition and structural characteristics of oxide scales formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloy systems.

This scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrograph shows the surface morphology of Fe-7.5 wt% Mn-5 wt% Al alloy after 24 hours of isothermal oxidation at 800°C in 200 torr pure oxygen, with a 130 μm scale bar provided for size reference. It features a large, rough-textured agglomerated oxide nodule at the center, alongside numerous smaller oxide nodules of varying sizes distributed across the darker underlying scale surface. As reported in the study, this alloy composition sits at the transition point (~7.5 wt% Mn) where oxide nodules shift from Fe-rich (large nodules, with outer Fe₂O₃ layer and Mn/Al enriched inner Fe₃O₄ layer) to Mn-rich (small nodules with outer manganese oxide layer), so both nodule types are observed here. This figure directly supports the conclusion that 7.5 wt% Mn is the critical transition concentration for oxide nodule composition and morphology in Fe-Mn-Al alloys oxidized at 800°C.

This is a cross-sectional micrograph showing discrete small oxide nodules formed on Fe–10Mn–5Al alloy after isothermal oxidation for 24 h at 800°C under 200 torr pure oxygen, with a 7 μm scale bar provided for size reference. The lower bright region corresponds to the Fe-Mn-Al alloy matrix, the upper dark region is the sample mounting medium, and the irregular raised features at the interface are the oxide nodules. As reported in the supporting text and caption, these small nodules (formed in alloys with Mn content >7.5 wt%) consist of an outer grey manganese oxide layer at the gas-oxide interface, an underlying white Fe₂O₃ layer, dispersed Al-rich phase particles inside the oxide, and a continuous Al-rich layer at the nodule-alloy interface. This figure validates that Mn content above ~7.5 wt% leads to formation of Mn-rich small oxide nodules instead of Fe-rich large nodules, supporting the compositional dependence of oxide scale morphology in high-temperature oxidized Fe-Mn-Al alloy systems.

This figure contains two micrographs characterizing the oxidized Fe-15Mn-6Al alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 800 °C in 200 torr pure oxygen. The first micrograph presents the cross-sectional morphology: the dark left region is the alloy matrix, while elongated bulky Mn-rich oxide phases grow outward from the alloy/scale interface toward the scale side on the right, which are confirmed to form above precipitated austenite in the matrix. The second micrograph shows the surface morphology of the oxidized alloy, with a high density of elongated, worm-like thread-shaped Mn-rich oxides distributed across the surface, exhibiting rough and uneven surfaces. This figure directly supports the conclusion that austenite in duplex Fe-Mn-Al alloys promotes the formation of bulky non-protective Mn-rich oxides during high-temperature oxidation, leading to deteriorated oxidation resistance.

This cross-sectional micrograph shows the microstructure of Fe–20Mn–6Al alloy after isothermal oxidation for 24 h at 800 °C in 200 torr pure oxygen, with a 20 μm scale bar provided. The top dark region represents bulky, non-protective manganese-rich oxide scales formed during oxidation. Beneath the oxide scale, a bright austenite-depleted ferrite surface layer is observed at the alloy-oxide interface, while the lower region shows the dendritic as-cast microstructure of the alloy matrix composed of ferrite and austenite phases. This figure confirms that alloys with high austenite content form stratified Mn-rich oxide scales above the secondary ferrite surface layer, verifying the detrimental effect of austenite on oxidation resistance: austenite promotes breakdown of pre-existing alumina scales and accelerates growth of bulky manganese-rich oxides, leading to poor high-temperature oxidation performance of the alloy.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents surface morphologies of oxidized Fe-Mn-Al alloys, with a 20 μm scale bar for both subgraphs. Subgraph (a) shows the surface of Fe-20Mn-8Al alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 800 °C in pure oxygen, where discrete, clustered worm-like manganese oxide threads are distributed on the alumina scale substrate. Subgraph (b) shows the surface of Fe-30Mn-8Al alloy under the same oxidation condition, with much denser, almost continuous manganese oxide threads covering a larger proportion of the surface. The comparison reveals that increasing Mn content from 20 wt% to 30 wt% significantly promotes the formation and coverage of manganese oxide threads, which grow through the preformed alumina scale. These morphologies confirm that higher Mn content and the presence of austenite in the alloy matrix degrade the protectiveness of alumina scales, facilitating the growth of Mn-rich oxides, supporting the core conclusion that austenite is detrimental to the oxidation resistance of duplex Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images with a unified 7 μm scale bar presents surface morphologies of spalled alumina regions and void formation on Fe-Mn-Al alloys after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 800 °C in pure oxygen. Subfigure (a) corresponds to Fe-25Mn-8Al, showing angular voids and Mn-rich oxide aggregates; Subfigure (b) for Fe-30Mn-9Al exhibits smooth rounded voids, wavy surface features, and bulky Mn-rich oxides; Subfigure (c) for Fe-30Mn-12Al displays rounded voids partially or fully filled with manganese oxides. The observed void morphology variation (angular vs rounded, unfilled vs Mn-oxide filled) with alloy Mn and Al content provides direct evidence supporting the proposed mechanism that Mn vapor diffusion drives void formation and Mn-rich oxide nodule growth through alumina scale defects, clarifying oxidation performance differences of Fe-Mn-Al alloys with varied compositions.

This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image showing the surface morphology of oxide protrusions formed on Fe-15wt%Mn-8wt%Al alloy after isothermal oxidation for 24 hours at 800 °C in 200 torr pure oxygen, with a 3 μm scale bar at the bottom right for size reference. Abundant discrete, agglomerated Mn-rich oxide protrusions with varying sizes from submicron to nearly 2 μm are observed distributing on the underlying alumina scale surface. As stated in the paper, such protrusions are rare on Fe-Mn-Al alloys with low Mn content, but become abundant when Mn concentration is high. This figure confirms that high Mn concentration in the alloy facilitates the growth of Mn-rich oxide nodules through the preformed alumina scale, supporting the conclusion that excess Mn degrades the protective performance of alumina scales on Fe-Mn-Al alloys at high temperature.

This figure presents an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping result for a small oxide nodule generated on Fe-15Mn-13Al alloy (run 11) after isothermal oxidation under 200 torr pure oxygen at the tested high temperature. The bright regions represent areas of high concentration of the mapped element, showing significant manganese enrichment within the oxide nodule, while an aluminum-rich oxide layer is confirmed to exist at the interface between the oxide nodule and the alloy substrate. This observation directly verifies that for Fe-Mn-Al alloys with aluminum content higher than 9 wt%, manganese-containing oxide nodules can penetrate the initially formed protective alumina scale during oxidation, supporting the proposed oxidation behavior mechanism for high-aluminum Fe-Mn-Al alloys in this work.

This figure includes two subplots characterizing oxide scales formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloys with >9 wt% Al (Region IV alloys) after isothermal oxidation in 200 torr pure oxygen at elevated temperatures. The first subplot is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) cross-sectional micrograph, showing the morphology of the alumina scale with small oxide nodules penetrating through the alumina layer, alongside a dark Al-rich oxide layer at the nodule-alloy interface. The second subplot is an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental map for Mn, displaying strong Mn enrichment localized exclusively at the nodule positions, verifying the manganese oxide composition of these protrusions. The results confirm that Mn-rich oxide phases penetrate otherwise protective alumina scales on high-Al Fe-Mn-Al alloys, even for alloys with <5 wt% bulk Mn content, supporting the oxidation behavior classification for Region IV of the 800°C Fe-Mn-Al alloy oxide map.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images displays the surface oxide morphology of Fe-10wt%Mn-6wt%Al alloy after 100 h isothermal oxidation in 200 torr pure oxygen at varied temperatures. The first subgraph corresponds to the sample oxidized at 800 °C, which shows degraded oxide scales with lifting, buckling features and localized bulky Mn-rich oxide nodules grown at discrete surface sites. The second subgraph corresponds to the sample oxidized at 1000 °C, exhibiting thick convoluted alumina scales that adhere well to the alloy substrate except for partial detachment at specimen edges, with scale thickness far higher than that of samples oxidized at lower temperatures. The third subgraph presents circular crater-like defective features on the oxidized surface, corresponding to localized oxide spallation or heterogeneous oxide growth sites. This figure demonstrates the significant temperature dependence of oxide scale structure for Fe-Mn-Al alloys, supporting that higher oxidation temperature facilitates alumina scale thickening, while oxidation at 800 °C induces partial scale degradation and non-protective Mn-rich oxide formation.

This figure contains two scanning electron microscopy images showing surface morphologies of Fe–7.5Mn–4Al–5Cr alloy samples after isothermal oxidation at 800°C in 200 torr pure oxygen. The left image displays a hollow, porous oxide mound formed on the alloy surface after 24 hr of oxidation, with abundant voids distributed across the mound surface. The right image presents the oxidized alloy surface after 100 hr of oxidation, showing an agglomerated, rough bulk oxide cluster, alongside filament-shaped manganese oxide threads located next to regions of spalled alumina scale. These observations verify that the 4 wt% Al content in this alloy is insufficient to form a stable continuous protective alumina scale, and scale degradation progresses with extended oxidation duration. The oxide threads support the conclusion that aluminum depletion of the substrate below the alumina scale induces austenite formation, which promotes the growth of Mn-rich oxide features even in Cr-containing Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

This figure contains two subgraphs for the Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr quaternary alloy oxidized under 200 torr pure oxygen at 800 °C for 24 h. The first subgraph shows the cross-sectional morphology of the formed oxide scale, which is highly convoluted and partially detached from the fully ferritic alloy substrate, with Mn depletion occurring at the substrate surface and Al enrichment at the scale base. The second subgraph is the Mn X-ray element distribution map (EDS mapping), which confirms that Mn is highly enriched in the thicker sections of the oxide scale, while Mn concentration in other scale areas is similar to that in the bulk alloy. This figure supports the conclusion that austenite in duplex Fe-Mn-Al based alloys induces the growth of bulky Mn-rich oxides and breaks pre-existing protective alumina scales, and reveals that the scale grown on Cr-containing Fe-Mn-Al alloys has uneven thickness with Mn enrichment sites originating from oxides formed above precipitated austenite.

This schematic illustrates three types of oxide nodule structures formed on Fe-Mn-Al alloys after oxidation at 800 °C. Type 1 corresponds to large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys with <5 wt% Mn, consisting of an outer Fe₂O₃ layer, inner (Fe,Mn)₃O₄ phase, and internal oxide region containing (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄ or Al₂O₃, with Al₂O₃ present on the base alloy surface. Type 2 refers to large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys with 5~7.5 wt% Mn, featuring discontinuous Mn₂O₃ layers/particles on the outermost surface, Fe₂O₃ as the sub-layer, (Fe,Mn)₃O₄ inside, and a partial healing layer of (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄ or Al₂O₃ at the nodule-alloy interface. Type 3 are small Mn-rich nodules formed on alloys with ≥7.5 wt% Mn, with a continuous outer Mn₂O₃ layer, inner Fe₂O₃ phase, and a complete healing layer of (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄ or Al₂O₃ at the base. This figure demonstrates that alloy Mn content determines nodule structure: higher Mn promotes continuous Mn₂O₃ outer layers and complete alumina/aluminate healing layers, retarding further nodule growth and improving oxidation resistance.

This set of schematic diagrams illustrates the five sequential growth stages of bulky manganese-rich oxides during oxidation of duplex α (ferrite) + γ (austenite) Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 800 °C under 200 torr oxygen pressure. Stage 1: a protective Al₂O₃ scale forms on the initial single α Fe-Mn-Al ferrite matrix. Stage 2: γ austenite plates nucleate and grow underneath the intact Al₂O₃ scale. Stage 3: worm-like Mn₂O₃ oxide threads grow outward from weak points of the overlying Al₂O₃ scale. Stage 4: the oxide threads and original Al₂O₃ scale are incorporated into the growing bulky Mn-rich oxide, which has an inner Fe₂O₃ layer and outer Mn₂O₃ layer, accompanied by Mn depletion that converts underlying γ austenite back to α ferrite. Stage 5: a continuous healing layer of Al₂O₃ or (Fe, Mn)AlO eventually forms at the oxide-alloy interface. The schematic demonstrates that the presence of austenite is detrimental to oxidation resistance, as it triggers breakdown of pre-existing protective alumina scales and promotes growth of non-protective bulky Mn-rich oxides, supporting the proposed oxidation mechanism for duplex Fe-Mn-Al alloys.

This figure contains two subplots focused on oxide scale structures of oxidized Fe-Mn-Al alloys. Subplot (a) is a cross-sectional micrograph with a 10 μm scale bar, showing the scale formed on Fe–25Mn–7Al alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 800 °C under 200 torr pure oxygen, with labeled oxide phases in regions 1–7: Mn₃O₄, (Fe,Mn)₃O₄, (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄, and Mn₂O₃. Subplot (b) is a schematic illustrating three oxide structure types for Fe-Mn-Al alloys with Mn content >20 wt%: Type 1 contains outer Mn₂O₃, inner Mn₃O₄ and (Fe,Mn)₃O₄ with incomplete interfacial aluminate layers; Type 2 has Mn₂O₃ over Mn₃O₄ with incomplete aluminate layers; Type 3 is single-phase Mn₂O₃ with a complete (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄ interfacial layer. The figure confirms that scale phase composition and structure depend on Mn enrichment and interfacial aluminate layer formation, supporting that high-Mn alloys form Mn-rich oxide scales when Al content is insufficient to form continuous protective alumina.

This 6-stage schematic illustrates the growth process of small manganese oxide nodules during oxidation of α and α+β-Mn Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 800°C under 200 torr pure oxygen. Stage (1) shows void formation beneath the pre-existing Al₂O₃ scale; Stage (2) depicts flaw formation in the alumina scale, where gaseous Mn reacts with infiltrated O₂(g); Stage (3) presents outward growth of Mn₂O₃ above the scale defect; Stage (4) demonstrates void filling via short-circuit oxygen diffusion, with Mn²⁺ outward and O²⁻ inward diffusion forming Mn₃O₄ at the lower part of the nodule; Stage (5) shows Al³⁺ diffusion from the alloy substrate leading to formation of Al₂O₃ or (Fe,Mn)Al₂O₄ particles at the oxide-metal interface of the filled void; Stage (6) shows formation of a protective healing layer, with the nodule mostly converted to Mn₂O₃. This figure supports the proposed oxidation mechanism that Mn-rich nodules penetrate alumina scales on high-Al Fe-Mn-Al alloys, and the formed alumina/spinel healing layer reduces subsequent oxidation rate after void filling.
